unit.name: Adding positional information and nonlinearity
unit.description: Shows why raw self-attention is insufficient and adds positional encodings plus a position-wise feed-forward network.
unit.summary: A central defect of plain self-attention is that it is permutation-invariant over the input set: the lecture uses "Zuko made his uncle" versus "his uncle made Zuko" to show that the same token multiset would otherwise get the same representations [ts=1306s] [ts=1352s]. To restore order, positional vectors p_i are added to token embeddings, with two options discussed: sinusoidal position encodings and learned position embeddings; learned embeddings are simpler and common in practice, but hard-limit the maximum sequence length and can fail on longer inputs [ts=1378s] [ts=1531s] [ts=1601s]. A second issue is that stacked self-attention layers without extra processing mostly keep re-averaging value vectors, so transformers add a position-wise feed-forward MLP after attention to inject nonlinearity and more expressive computation per token [ts=1830s] [ts=1867s] [ts=1945s].

key_points.text: Plain self-attention has no notion of sequence order and acts on a set of token vectors.
"Zuko made his uncle" and "his uncle made Zuko" illustrate that token order must be encoded explicitly.
A positional vector p_i can be added to the word embedding x_i so the model knows both token identity and index.
Sinusoidal position encodings vary by dimension and period, but the instructor notes they do not reliably solve extrapolation in practice.
Learned position embeddings are common but impose a maximum supported sequence length n.
A position-wise feed-forward MLP after self-attention adds the nonlinear processing missing from repeated weighted averaging.

unit_summary_for_generation: A central defect of plain self-attention is that it is permutation-invariant over the input set: the lecture uses "Zuko made his uncle" versus "his uncle made Zuko" to show that the same token multiset would otherwise get the same representations [ts=1306s] [ts=1352s]. To restore order, positional vectors p_i are added to token embeddings, with two options discussed: sinusoidal position encodings and learned position embeddings; learned embeddings are simpler and common in practice, but hard-limit the maximum sequence length and can fail on longer inputs [ts=1378s] [ts=1531s] [ts=1601s]. A second issue is that stacked self-attention layers without extra processing mostly keep re-averaging value vectors, so transformers add a position-wise feed-forward MLP after attention to inject nonlinearity and more expressive computation per token [ts=1830s] [ts=1867s] [ts=1945s].

kp_catalog.name: Positional encoding
kp_catalog.description: Because self-attention alone ignores order, position information must be injected, commonly by adding sinusoidal or learned positional vectors to token embeddings. Because self-attention on its own does not care about order beyond corresponding shuffles, positional embeddings are added when order information matters.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because positional encoding clarifies because self-attention alone ignores order, position information must be injected, commonly by adding sinusoidal or learned positional vectors to token embeddings, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Position-wise feed-forward network
kp_catalog.description: A per-token MLP applied after attention adds nonlinearity and extra compute so stacked layers do more than repeated weighted averaging.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because position-wise feed-forward network clarifies a per-token MLP applied after attention adds nonlinearity and extra compute so stacked layers do more than repeated weighted averaging, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
